sections.text: Welcome back to AP Computer Science A. Module Seven — ArrayList. We're moving beyond fixed-size arrays into a collection that grows and shrinks as your program runs. This is the single most useful container in the Java standard library, and the College Board expects you to know it cold.

Arrays are great — but their size is fixed forever. The moment you write int array bracket ten, you have ten slots, no more, no less. Need to add a student mid-semester? You can't. You have to allocate a bigger array and copy. ArrayList solves this. It grows on demand. It shrinks on demand. Same indexing, none of the pain.

Three things today. One — how ArrayList differs from a plain array, in size, syntax, and what it can hold. Two — the six methods you'll use ninety-nine percent of the time: add, get, set, remove, size, and the two-argument insert. Three — iteration with both kinds of for loops, and the autoboxing pitfall around the Integer wrapper.

First — the import. ArrayList lives in the java dot util package. You must import it before you can use it. Either name the class directly with import java dot util dot ArrayList, or pull in the whole package with import java dot util dot star. On the AP exam, either is fine — but most professional code names the class explicitly.

Declaring an ArrayList uses generics — the angle bracket syntax. ArrayList of String, names, equals new ArrayList of empty angle brackets. The empty diamond on the right tells Java to infer the type from the left. Before Java seven you had to repeat the type — ArrayList of String on both sides. The diamond is cleaner.

Quick word on generics. Generics let a collection hold any type while staying type-safe. ArrayList of String only accepts Strings. ArrayList of Integer only accepts Integers. The compiler enforces this, so you catch type bugs before your code ever runs. One catch — primitives like int and double can't go inside. You need their wrapper classes, Integer and Double.

Six methods to memorize. List dot add of x appends x to the end. List dot add of i comma x inserts x at index i, shifting everything right. List dot get of i returns the element at index i. List dot set of i comma x replaces what's at index i. List dot remove of i deletes index i, shifting everything left. And list dot size returns the count. Note the parentheses on size — it's a method, not a field.

Your turn. Create an ArrayList of String. Add the strings A, B, and C in order. Then remove the element at index one. Then print what's left. What's in the list? Pause now, write the four lines, press play when you're ready.

Walk-through. ArrayList of String list equals new ArrayList. Then list dot add of A, list dot add of B, list dot add of C. Then list dot remove of one — that removes B, because B was at index one. Everything shifts left. The result is A and C, size two.

Two ways to iterate. First — the indexed for loop. For int i equals zero, i less than list dot size, i plus plus, print list dot get of i. Pay attention to that detail. ArrayList uses dot size with parentheses — it's a method. Plain arrays use dot length with no parentheses — it's a field. Mix them up and the compiler will tell you immediately.

Second — the enhanced for loop. For String s colon list, print s. Cleaner, more readable when you just want to look at each element. But one warning. You can't add to or remove from the list while you're inside an enhanced for loop. Try it and Java throws ConcurrentModificationException. Use the indexed loop when you need to modify.

Side by side. Array is fixed size — set once, never changes. ArrayList grows and shrinks freely. Array uses dot length as a field. ArrayList uses dot size as a method. Arrays are slightly faster because there's no growth bookkeeping. Arrays can hold primitives directly, like int bracket. ArrayLists can only hold objects, so primitives need wrapper types.

About those wrappers. Autoboxing is Java automatically converting between primitive and wrapper. ArrayList of Integer nums. Nums dot add of five — that five is auto-boxed into an Integer object. Int x equals nums dot get of zero — that Integer is auto-unboxed back into an int. Convenient, but watch one thing. If you unbox a null Integer, Java throws NullPointerException. Always check before unboxing.

Recap. ArrayList grows and shrinks unlike fixed arrays. Import it from java dot util. Declare with the angle-bracket type. Six core methods — add, two-argument add, get, set, remove, size. Use dot size with parentheses, not dot length. ArrayList holds objects only, so primitives are autoboxed into wrappers. Your assignment is in the dashboard.

This video is the overview. To actually master ArrayList, here's the rest. Read the ArrayList chapter on Runestone — it has interactive code blocks. Practice the AP-One ArrayList problem set on Codingbat. Submit the reverse-printing assignment in your dashboard. And book a check-in with your advisor if any of this still feels fuzzy. Next up — Module Eight, two-D arrays.
